headlines.title: Bicyclist dies after collision with vehicle in San Jose
headlines.description: A bicyclist was killed in a vehicle collision in San Jose Tuesday morning, according to police. Officers are investigating the fatal crash in the area of North Capitol Avenue and Gay Avenue. Police said the adult male bicyclist was pronounced dead at the scene. Northbound lanes of North Capitol Avenue are closed between Madden Avenue and McKee Road as investigators process the scene. Police said the closures are expected to remain in place for several hours. Drivers are being urged to avoid the area and use alternate routes. Additional information was not immediately available.

headlines.title: 2 pedestrians fatally struck in San Francisco identified
headlines.description: Two San Francisco pedestrians who died in separate traffic collisions last week were identified Monday by the Office of the Chief Medical Examiner. Cheryl Coleman, a 61-year-old San Francisco resident, was identified as the victim of a collision in the city’s South of Market neighborhood. At 5:31 a.m. on Wednesday, San Francisco police responded to a reported collision around Brannan and Seventh streets. According to police, Coleman was pronounced dead at the scene. The driver of the vehicle that hit her stayed and cooperated with the investigation, and police did not find evidence of alcohol or drugs, the department said. Less than an hour later, police and paramedics responded to the 900 block of Geneva Avenue in the Excelsior neighborhood to investigate a report about a deceased person in the street. Edson Abraham Morales Garay, a 26-year-old San Francisco resident, was identified by the medical examiner as the victim in that collision. The pedestrian advocacy nonprofit Walk San Francisco said this collision was the third pedestrian death on Geneva Avenue in the last three years. “Our hearts break for the victims and their loved ones,” said Jodie Medeiros, executive director of Walk SF, in a statement. “People are dying simply walking in our city.”

headlines.title: 2 dead after crash involving reckless driver, Oakland police officer
headlines.description: Two people died and a police officer was injured Monday when a reckless driver crashed into an Oakland police vehicle, according to officials and sources. The crash happened at about 11 a.m. near East 12th Street and 29th Avenue, according to the Oakland Housing Authority (OHA). The housing authority did not immediately provide specifics on the two fatalities or say if anyone else was injured. Oakland Councilmember Noel Gallo said the impact of the crash killed two passengers in the vehicle driven by the reckless driver. That driver tried to flee on foot after the collision, but they were taken into custody. Sources with direct knowledge of the investigation said the officer involved in the crash was transported to a local hospital. They are expected to recover. An Oakland Housing Authority Police Department (OHAPD) officer initially spotted a vehicle driving recklessly in the area of East 12th Street, OHA said. The officer tried to pull the driver over, but the driver took off at a high rate of speed. The officer turned off his emergency lights and siren and did not chase after the driver, a move consistent with OHAPD policy, OHA said. Shortly thereafter, the driver crashed into an Oakland Police Department (OPD) vehicle. An investigation is ongoing. Additional information wasn’t immediately available.

headlines.title: Historic Pacifica Municipal Pier damage leads to demolition of popular cafe
headlines.description: It’s the end of an era along the coastline in Pacifica. The historic Pacifica Municipal Pier, built more than 50 years ago, is crumbling under the pressure of the powerful and erosive Pacific surf, leading to a local emergency. The decades-old Chit Chat Cafe at the base of the pier is now being pulled apart as the pier separates from the shoreline, and on Monday night, Pacifica’s City Council unanimously approved its demolition. Pacifica Jun 6 Pacifica Jun 4 Pacifica May 20 Pacifica residents said the community will lose parts of its very identity with the demolition of the iconic sites. “This is just really so sad,” Pacifica resident Pam Coppin said. “And it was a nice thing that people liked to come and hang out here.” The 1,100 foot pier was built in 1973 and became a popular attraction for tourists and fishermen. After winter storms damaged it two years ago, the City of Pacifica paid nearly $20 million for repairs to the pier. The city declared a public emergency and closed the pier last week after the new cracks formed. Pacifica has also spoken openly about possibly demolishing the pier, but it has not yet declared what it intendeds to do. As crews worked throughout the day Monday to assess the structural integrity of the pier and nearby cafe, city officials said serious damage was found near the pier abutment that anchors the pier to land. For Chit Chat Cafe owner Ginger Davis, the loss is deeply personal. Davis and her husband met while working at the cafe two decades ago and bought the business three years ago. “The whole fishing community is losing something gigantic,” Davis said. “We love them. Those are our customers. We would see some of them every day.” The local emergency declaration could help unlock federal funding, but that could still face turbulent waters.
headlines.source: Thom Jensen and Ginger Conejero Saab

headlines.title: San Francisco's beloved Wave Organ turns 40
headlines.description: San Francisco’s beloved Wave Organ, a strange creation of science and art in the city’s Marina District, is marking its 40th anniversary on Monday. The piece officially opened on June 8, 1986, crafted by artist Peter Richards and stone mason George Gonzalez. On a narrow inlet that winds past the San Francisco Yacht Club, the piece was crafted around Richards’ concept of extending 20 pipes into the waves with visitors able to listen to the gurgling on the other end. Gonzalez, a local stone mason trained in France, built terraces and sculptures using stones rescued from the bay or recycled from a former cemetery. “I just wanted to capture the sound at first,” said Richards, sitting in the small stone vestibule at the site. “At the same time, particularly working with George, we wanted to create a place that was special so that people come here and have a different kind of experience.” The piece was funded by the Exploratorium, where Richards worked at the time, back when the science museum was located in the nearby Palace of Fine Arts. Richards took inspiration from an exhibit in the museum called Pipes of Pan, which demonstrated how different length tubes amplify sound waves of different frequencies. “Grabbed a few pipes and came out,” Richards said. “Stuck the pipes in the water and there it was, this very musical gurgling.” The Wave Organ in San Francisco’s Marina opened 40 years ago Monday. The two creators visited the site last for the first time in many years. They plunked down in the vestibule where small openings connected to pipes out in the bay. The various lengths of tube change the sound, which is most audible during high tides. “It felt like we got a permit from God to build this,” Gonzalez said, taking in the scene. “When I came here I went, ‘Oh my God, this is a fantastic spot.’ It’s a dream place to leave your work.” Richards first learned of Gonzalez after admiring a stone bench Gonzalez had created outside Smiley’s Saloon in the West Marin town of Bolinas. He tracked down the stone mason and offered him the project. Gonzalez, with aid from the California Conservation Corps, used nets to harvest cobblestones from the jetty, which were originally used as ballast for sailing vessels. He also got materials from a city salvage yard, which included many ornate carved stones that were once part of the Laurel Hill Cemetery, relocated in 1939. The old stones give the site the look of an ancient ruined empire — while the tubes transmitting the wave sounds look more like periscopes from a comic book submarine. “That makes me feel really good that somehow I had a hand in creating a place that allows people to be themselves and create their own stories,” Richards said, admiring the site. The piece has drawn people for 40 years – some who stumble on it by accident, many who seek it out. For locals, visits feel like an insider’s scoop on a secret destination. The soundtrack of the bay and the mysterious gurgling feels remote, even though it directly faces the bustling city. “I’ve been out here at three in the morning and it’s fantastic,” said Ken Finn, an Exploratorium educator who leads studies of the wave organ. “If someone asked me the best time to come here, I think my first answer is anytime.” The creators of the Wave Organ, Peter Richards and George Gonzalez, visit the site they built. Richards said years ago there was a proposal to redo the yacht harbor, which would’ve shifted the bay waters in the channel and render the Wave Organ useless. Richards said he turned down an offer to relocate the piece across the channel. He said what made the Wave Organ magic was its remote location. “It’s strange, it’s wonderful,” Richards said. “It’s not really land but it’s not really sea. It encompasses both.” Forty years after its opening, the Wave Organ is essentially the same as when it opened. The one big change is 15 years ago when a fan of the project left money to build a ramp to make it ADA accessible. The structure also survived the Loma Prieta earthquake, which moved one stone a couple inches. “I was 38 years old when I built this,” recalled Gonzalez, who says he guaranteed the work for 500 years. “It’s endured well and that’s kind of like where I’m looking at it. This really stood the test of time so far.” In today’s world of increased restrictions and permits, it’s difficult to imagine someone being allowed to build a permanent project of such magnitude. As Finn pressed his ear against one of the tubes to listen to the musical gurgling, he marveled that the project even exists. “I’m really grateful they had the foresight and tenacity to get through all the different hoops and hurdles to get this built and to build it in a way that it’ll be here for a long time,” Finn said. “Hopefully 40 more years.” The Wave Organ in San Francisco’s Marina opened 40 years ago Monday.

headlines.title: OpenAI files for IPO as AI investment race intensifies
headlines.description: OpenAI announced Monday afternoon that it has filed for an initial public offering of stock, just a week after its chief rival, Anthropic did the same. “We recently submitted a confidential S-1,” the ChatGPT maker said in a post on X, referring to the formal name of the filing. “We have not decided on timing yet; it may be a while because there are things we want to do that are likely easier as a private company.” The artificial intelligence startup added that “it’s a complicated set of tradeoffs and this gives us the option to go public sooner if that ends up being best.” In March, OpenAI’s valuation soared to $852 billion after it raised a fresh $122 billion in cash to fuel its ever-expanding ambitions in AI. That cash is being used to develop advanced AI models, but also the extensive physical data center infrastructure and cloud computing capacity required to run those systems. Apple Inc. Jun 8 SPACEX Jun 3 On June 1, OpenAI’s primary rival Anthropic also filed for a public stock offering. That startup was worth $952 billion in its most recent funding round. Regardless when OpenAI and Anthropic decide to begin trading this year, they will be the second and third IPOs to follow SpaceX’s trillion-dollar offering, which is set for Friday. Elon Musk’s rocket company is also heavily involved in artificial intelligence, through its xAI business.

headlines.title: East Bay family's deep ties with the Oakland Roots soccer team
headlines.description: Soccer is a community. It also creates opportunities. In the East Bay, one family’s bond with the Oakland Roots soccer team goes beyond the field. The Gonzalez family says the team betted on them and will now consider them part of the family. They have four generations with a connection to the soccer club since its inaugural season in 2019. NBC Bay Area’s Janelle Wang has more in the video report above. soccer Jun 4 soccer Jun 5 Diego Luna's passion for fútbol starts with family

headlines.title: Man dead, woman injured after shooting outside Kaiser facility in San Jose
headlines.description: A man died and a woman was hospitalized Monday following a shooting outside a medical facility in South San Jose, according to police. The shooting happened outside the Kaiser Permanente medical office campus in the area of Cottle Road and Beswick Drive. Officers responded to the facility at about 10:14 a.m. and found the man, who police said is believed to be the suspect in the shooting, and the woman, a Kaiser Permanente employee, each suffering from at least one gunshot wound. The man was taken to a local hospital, where he later died, police said. The woman was hospitalized in critical but stable condition. Police said the man and the woman knew each other, but the exact nature of their relationship wasn’t immediately known. Detectives were working to determine a motive for the shooting. In a statement, Kaiser Permanente said, in part: “This was a personal matter, with the shooter coming to our employee’s place of work. While the isolated event is deeply concerning, there is no ongoing threat to our employees, physicians, patients, members, or visitors.” Police said the shooting was believed to be an isolated incident, adding that there was no ongoing threat to public safety. An investigation is ongoing. This story uses functionality that may not work in our app. Click here to open the story in your web browser.

headlines.title: Apple unveils an upgraded Siri voice assistant with new AI features at its annual conference
headlines.description: Apple on Monday unveiled new artificial intelligence advances including upgrades to its Siri assistant, emphasizing a focus on privacy and day-to-day use as the iPhone maker tries to catch up to rivals when it comes to AI. Siri AI, which was introduced at the start of Apple’s annual World Wide Developers Conference, has been highly anticipated by users and developers. While Siri was launched in 2011, it fell behind other voice assistants and was derided even by Apple fans. It is the last WWDC featuring CEO Tim Cook before he turns his post over to John Ternus in September. Cook received an extended standing ovation and told the audience he is “deeply grateful to have been on this journey with you” and said “the energy around Apple platforms has never been stronger.” The conference, which drew developers from some 65 countries to Apple’s Silicon Valley headquarters, focuses on software, in contrast to the fall unveiling of the latest iPhones. Apple steps up its AI game Apple has sought to distinguish itself from its peers by stressing a privacy-centered approach and integrating AI across its devices and apps. There was no mention of AI superintelligence or companion chatbots. Rather, Apple sought to demonstrate how its AI tools can make life easier. Apple software chief Craig Federighi took some swipes at AI companies — without naming them — that seem to be “pursuing AI for the sake of AI” without clear regard for the people it is supposed to serve. At Apple, he said, “we believe that truly helpful AI should be centered around you and your needs,” which means integrating AI into the products people use every day, with a focus on privacy. The conference unveiled updates to Apple Intelligence, which was first announced in 2024. It now uses Google’s Gemini AI model to help power its features and processes users’ requests and interactions on their own device and privately on the cloud, making them inaccessible to anyone else. At the center of it is the new Siri, which Apple said is now a “much more capable assistant” that can help users find what they need and get things done across various Apple devices. For instance, it can create a menu and gather recipes from the web or from your own text messages for a World Cup viewing party and invite friends from a group chat. Siri mode on your camera, meanwhile, can tell you what you are looking at and give you relevant information, such as the nutritional details of a plate of food. Siri’s visual intelligence also works with images on your screen. For example, it can tell you whether a backpack you are thinking of getting will work as a carry-on for a flight or whether a pair of bulky hiking boots will fit inside it. Apple also announced improvements to its popular AI photo editing tools, including spatial reframing that lets you adjust how a photo is framed after it was taken — as if you had moved the camera to a better position while you were snapping the picture. A standalone Siri AI app will launch later this year, though Apple said it will not initially be available in Europe and it won’t be available in China while the company works out regulatory issues. Tim Cook’s last WWDC Cook announced his retirement in April, ending a 15-year run that saw the company’s market value soar by more than $4 trillion during an iPhone-fueled era of prosperity. Ternus has been with Apple for the past quarter century, including the past five years overseeing the engineering underlying the iPhone, iPad and Mac — a role that made him a prime candidate to succeed Cook. Ternus did not take the main stage during Monday’s event. The transition to a new CEO comes at a pivotal time for Apple. Artificial intelligence has unleashed the most upheaval within the industry since Jobs unveiled the first iPhone in 2007. Apple has gotten off to a rough start in AI after stumbling in its efforts to deliver new features built on the technology, as promised nearly two years ago. Cook called his time at Apple “the honor of a lifetime.” “I truly believe the best is still ahead.”

headlines.title: East Bay motorcyclist killed in Highway 9 crash in Santa Cruz County
headlines.description: A Martinez motorcyclist was killed Sunday in rural Santa Cruz County after his bike veered off the road and struck a tree, the California Highway Patrol said. According to the CHP, the 30-year-old man was driving his 2023 Ducati motorcycle at an unknown speed on state Highway 9 south of state Highway 35 when for reasons still under investigation, he traveled off the roadway and crashed into an embankment and a tree. The man suffered major injuries and was pronounced dead at the scene, the CHP said. It is unknown at this time if alcohol or drugs played a role in the crash, according to the CHP, but an investigation is ongoing.

headlines.title: 5 vehicles stolen from Alameda County parking garage in Oakland
headlines.description: Five vehicles were stolen from an Alameda County parking garage in Oakland Sunday morning, according to authorities. The sheriff’s office said personnel discovered that the Alameda County Parking Garage at 165 13th Street in Oakland was burglarized at 7:16 a.m., and five vehicles were stolen, including Alameda County vehicles and a sheriff’s office vehicle. One of the stolen vehicles was a rental car, the sheriff’s office said. Officials said investigators initially recovered four vehicles using automated license plate reader cameras, and they were looking for the fifth. By Sunday evening, authorities told NBC Bay Area the last outstanding vehicle was found. Sergeant Roberto Morales, Public Information Officer with the Alameda County Sheriff’s Office said, “the investigation is still going to continue.” Morales added, “We’re going to try and figure out what happened, probably set up looking into our security protocols, harden the site, see what happened, how did this happen, and trying to find out who did this, why they did this.” Morales said that the thief or theives forced entry into the gararge. Several Alameda County Supervisors told NBC Bay Area Sunday they are awaiting more information abotu this incident, but plan to look into what happened. EDITOR’S NOTE: Sheriff’s officials initially reported five sheriff’s office vehicles were stolen, but they later clarified that four of them were county vehicles and at least one was a sheriff’s office unmarked vehicle.

headlines.title: Bay Area readies for World Cup as kickoff nears
headlines.description: With the FIFA World Cup set to begin in just days, changes are well underway at Levi’s Stadium in Santa Clara. During the tournament, the home of the San Francisco 49ers will be known as San Francisco Bay Area Stadium, in accordance with FIFA sponsorship rules. Signage at the stadium has already been covered as part of the transition. The change has also appeared online. Apple Maps now lists the venue as San Francisco Bay Area Stadium to help visitors attending matches during the tournament. The World Cup will be played across the United States, Mexico and Canada. The tournament begins Thursday in Mexico City, with the Bay Area’s first match scheduled for Saturday, when Qatar faces Switzerland. On Monday, San Francisco Mayor Daniel Lurie hosted a World Cup kickoff event, stressing public safety and public transit as top priorities to make the World Cup a community event. In San Jose, Mayor Matt Mahan delivered a similar message at the city’s official World Cup kickoff event, saying the city will help its diverse communities gather in neighborhoods as well as at a central party downtown in San Pedro Square. Meanwhile, Team Paraguay arrived in the Bay Area over the weekend. The team landed at San Jose Mineta International Airport and will use San Jose State University as its base camp during the tournament. Paraguay’s first match is scheduled for Friday in Los Angeles against the U.S. men’s national team.
